body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: 01 Palimpsest, a new way to IETF Draft
02 Large Language Model (LLM) for Networking: an Architecture
03 KIRA – Scalable Zero-Touch Routing for Control Plane Fabrics
04 About Extrinsic, Intrinsic, Decentralized, and Stem Identifiers (UNS-UCA Update)
06 Formal Analysis of Attested TLS for Confidential Computing
08 Preventing denial of service attacks on TLS handshakes
10 A proposed SUSTAIN RG - Sustainability and the Internet

story that goes back 13 years I was using remote screen sharing to my Mac at home and and it was really sluggish and it was over a slow DSL line so maybe that's not surprising the network slow you expect it to be sluggish and this was 2011 I just learned about buffer bloat from Jim gtis so my first reaction was to blame the network oh it's buffer bloat in the network I did some investigation and in this case it wasn't uh but there was a two second lag between clicking on the menu and seeing it appear and when I started digging it's like those horror movies the phone was coming from inside the house the buffer bloat was on the computer itself it wasn't in the network so this is what was going on uh with BSD sockets as it was back then the screen sharing application would grab a frame compress it send it to TCP it would go into the socket sand buffer and TCP would say please sir can I have some more and the frame but the screen sharing would grab another frame compress it send it grab another frame compress it send it those frames weren't going out in the wire they were just sitting in kernel memory in the socket's end buffer waiting for tcp's congestion control algorithm to decide it was time and the network could handle that data so we had some data in Flight which is good to keep the bandwidth delay product full to make full use of the pipeline and a full two seconds of stale frames just sitting in Ram well this this isn't helping the user experience so to to combat this we came up with a socket option the TCP not sent low Watermark and the way this works is when an application like screen sharing is blocked waiting on K event for a socket to become writable TCP doesn't tell it that the socket's writable until it's actually getting close to needing that data and that simple fix I worked with the kernel team to put it in the kernel I then sat down one afternoon with the screen sharing engineer added this new socket option to screen sharing and it dramatically fixed the lag with screen sharing and this is the after picture you still have the blue data in flight to keep the band delay product full but you have a tiny bit of data waiting to go out and you don't actually invite the application to give you more data until you're ready for it so that's great there are two problems we've found one is that we specified the trigger point for the socket being writable as a low water mark in bytes and about 16 kilobytes about 10 ethernet frames works well for wide range of cases but at very low data rates 16 kilobytes is a lot of data it can be a second of latency and at multi- gigabit rates it's not enough time for the application to replenish data so we realize to be better to specify the delay in time the application is saying give me 20 milliseconds notice and I'll give you more data the other problem is this was also Implement on Linux and I was overjoyed I want this to be crossplatform I want all software to be better but it was done differently on Macos it is a low Watermark when the unsent data drains to that level you then trigger the application to send more on Linux it was implemented as a high Watermark it is the upper limit of what the application can send so for people writing applications it's the socket option with the same name but it has opposite Behavior so I think it's time for us to fix this I've scheduled
